openInvestigationDesc: Start from a suspicion — a vendor, institution, or pattern — and build a documented case with AI-powered analysis tools.
compranetContextTitle: The Platform This Database Came From No Longer Exists
compranetContextBody: CompraNet — Mexico's federal procurement portal and the source of all 3.05M contracts here — was abolished by Congress on April 10, 2025. Before any law changed, 1.9 million contracts from 2012–2023 had already been removed from public access in August 2024. The independent transparency watchdog INAI was constitutionally eliminated in December 2024. The 3.05M contracts in this database were captured before these changes and may represent one of the most complete surviving records of Mexico's federal procurement history.
aucExplanation: AUC 0.828: if given one contract from a confirmed corruption case and one random contract, the model correctly identifies the corrupt one 82.8 times out of 100.
yearSummaryNoData: No data available for this year
seasonalPatternsDesc: Monthly risk premium vs rest-of-year average
seasonalPatternsNote: December contracts show elevated risk -- budget pressure drives year-end spending
dashboardLoadError: Dashboard data failed to load. Some sections may be unavailable.
lede: 3.05M federal contracts analyzed (2002–2025) — 413K flagged as high or critical risk.
sectionLoadError: This section could not be loaded.
storyDecemberDesc: The average December contract (5.4M MXN) exceeds the rest-of-year average by 47% — the classic year-end budget dump that favors direct awards.
storyHaciendaDesc: In 2021, a single vendor (CFE) captured nearly 1 in 5 pesos from the Hacienda sector — 6.2B MXN out of a total 32.8B MXN.
storyDirectDesc: In 2023, 82.2% of all contracts were direct awards — a rise of +19.5pp from 62.7% in 2010. The highest rate in 23 years of data.
signalStripHighRiskRate: of contracts flagged high or critical risk

periodTrendDataQualityNote: ⚠ Pre-2010 data (Structure A): 0.1% RFC coverage — risk scores are directional estimates only, not comparable to later periods.
sitRepSubhead: RUBLI has analyzed {{contracts}} federal contracts worth {{totalValue}} across 23 years. {{riskValue}} sits in contracts flagged high or critical risk -- patterns consistent with documented corruption cases.
sectorChartHeadline: Salud and Agricultura lead in average contract risk
sectorChartSubhead: Average risk score per sector, based on v0.6.5 model across 3.05M contracts. Higher bars indicate greater concentration of known-corruption-like patterns.
sectorChartAriaLabel: Horizontal bar chart showing average risk score by federal sector
sectorChartSource: Source: RUBLI v0.6.5 risk model, 3,051,294 contracts (2002-2025). Risk scores are statistical indicators, not corruption probabilities.
sectorChartDataQualityNote: Data quality varies by period: 2002–2010 contracts have 0.1% vendor RFC coverage (lowest quality) — sector averages for that era are directional estimates. Coverage improves to 15.7% (2010–2017), 30.3% (2018–2022), and 47.4% (2023–2025).

daRecord.headline: The most opaque year in 23 years of data
daRecord.body: In the final full year of López Obrador's government, 82 of every 100 pesos in contracts went to companies that faced no competition — 3.3 times the OECD's recommended limit of 25%.

ghostCompanies.headline: Born in the 4T era. Won without competing.
ghostCompanies.body: Companies incorporated after 2018, with no prior federal contracting history, obtained more than 95% of their contracts through direct award and each accumulated over 10 million pesos.

segalmex.headline: The empty granary of the Fourth Transformation
segalmex.body: SEGALMEX, created in 2019 to feed the poor, operated with a 93.4% direct-award rate. The Superior Audit Office documented a 15 billion-peso shortfall. Its 22 main vendors average critical risk scores in the RUBLI model.

efos.statLabel: RFCs on SAT EFOS list — fictitious invoicing
efos.headline: The SAT confirmed it. The government hired them anyway.
efos.body: Article 69-B of the Tax Code requires publishing companies that issue fictitious invoices. As of March 2026, 13,960 are in 'definitive' status. RUBLI found that dozens held active government contracts after appearing on the list.

riskModel.statLabel: contracts at critical or high risk level
riskModel.headline: A system that flags. Investigators still needed.
riskModel.body: The RUBLI v0.6.5 model (AUC 0.828) assigns critical or high risk to 412,000 contracts. Each one is a lead. None is a verdict. They are the starting point for journalism and accountability.
